notes.run_04_and_04b: Same campaign (327b55e1e, 12-round Qwen3.5-4B/L40S) mined by two different agents with different schemas. run_04 has nested categories, run_04b has flat intervention_inventory. Both included but deduplicated in aggregate counts.
notes.runs_05_and_06: Same campaign (3ed2cc190, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B/L40S) mined by two different agents. Both included.
notes.runs_13_16: Runs 13-16 have partial campaign metrics (overall_score present, but rounds/shipped/speedup missing from top-level campaign_metrics). These are included for anti-pattern and qualitative data.
notes.citation_verification: Approximately 80% of citations verified exact-match; remaining 20% have fuzzy-match issues but underlying findings are real per verification JSONs.

intervention_taxonomy.description: Classification of all monitor interventions across sessions. Only runs 00, 04/04b, 08, 12, and 17 had active transcript monitors. The remaining 13 runs had monitors disabled or missing, providing natural counterfactual baselines.

evidence.description: Flagging invalid measurements, L2 cache artifacts, baseline mismatch, inflated speedup claims, unverified assumptions

methodology.description: Flawed benchmarking methodology, missing CUDA graphs, wrong output-len, premature optimization, missing GPU reservation

correctness.description: Kernel correctness failures, hardware misconceptions (FP8 MMA on SM89), tolerance masking bugs, runtime crashes

reasoning.description: Flawed Amdahl reasoning, occupancy misconceptions, overoptimistic BW targets, disproved hypotheses

coaching.description: Explicit coaching suggestions (e.g., Self-Validation Gate protocol after repeated failures)

other_and_unclassified.description: Interventions from run_17 and run_12 with topic-based rather than category-based schemas (e.g., 'L2 flush too small', 'wrong lm_head shape'). These map approximately to evidence (60%) and methodology (30%) but are not force-classified to preserve fidelity.

by_severity.note: Severity counts from runs with explicit severity fields. run_17 uses topic-based classification.

impact_distribution.description: Impact classification of interventions where formal counterfactual analysis was performed. run_00 has the most complete impact data (27 interventions with impact ratings). run_04b adds 16 more classified interventions.

before_actor_noticed.description: Whether monitor flagged the issue before the actor independently noticed it (from run_00 data, 27 interventions)
before_actor_noticed.note: 48% of interventions caught issues before the actor noticed. The remaining 52% served as confirmation or urgency reinforcement.

false_positive_rate.description: 3 NONE-impact interventions out of 43 classified (monitor incorrectly cited 5% threshold, timing concern that was 6 seconds off, GPU reservation procedural warning). These were self-corrected or harmless.

false_negative_assessment.description: Runs without monitors (01, 02, 03, 05, 06, 07, 09, 10, 11) had anti-patterns that monitors would likely have caught: hallucinated E2E improvements (run_01: 6.02% claimed vs 4.79% actual), unreported regressions (run_01: 2.63% BS=1 regression), cold-to-production overestimation in 10 runs. These provide counterfactual evidence that monitors reduce error propagation.
key_examples: run_01: Hallucinated 6.02% E2E (actual 4.79%) pushed past 5% kill criterion -- no monitor to flag
run_02: Same campaign re-mined independently found same inflation issue
run_07: Champion-3 benchmarked against wrong baseline (C++ instead of torch.compile) -- debate peer review caught it, but monitor would have caught it earlier
run_09: Both shipped ops failed kill criteria -- monitors would have escalated

aggregate.note: Aggregate stats computed over runs with non-null values for each field. run_04/04b counted once.

anti_pattern_frequency.description: Recurrence of failure modes across sessions. cold_to_production_overestimation is the dominant anti-pattern appearing in 10 of 18 runs (55.6%), confirming it as the most common failure mode for coding agents optimizing GPU kernels.

frequency_table.description: Debate E2E projections overestimate actual validated E2E by 1.5-7x. Root cause: cold-cache kernel speedup does not account for production L2 cache state, NCCL spin-wait absorption, or monolithic kernel fusion advantages.
Bottleneck analysis declares a component 'near-optimal' or 'no red flags' at BW utilizations below 85%, discouraging optimization of the dominant bottleneck component.
Champion fabricates or misattributes data (wrong layer counts, inflated E2E percentages, wrong f_decode values).
Repeated failures from the same root cause (wrong class patched, wrong output-len flags).
Kernel-level speedup (1.64x, 1.29x) fails to translate to E2E due to L2 cache effects or wave count scaling.
Champion benchmarks against wrong baseline (non-CUDA-graph mode, C++ kernel instead of torch.compile production).
Both shipped optimizations failed their kill criteria but were shipped anyway.
Champion's smoke test reference function had same bug as kernel (sigmoid vs silu), hiding correctness failure.
Champion dismissed Triton for 88.6% bottleneck based on single flawed experiment (BLOCK_M=16 hardcode).
Gate validator hook (329 LOC + 674 LOC tests) never fired during campaign.
Validator fabricated profiling data (ViT encoder attribution for text-only model).
All Round 1 implementations failed (100% washout), wasting full debate+implementation cycle.

anti_pattern_frequency.key_finding: cold_to_production_overestimation appears in 10 of 18 runs (55.6%), spanning all 4 models and both hardware platforms. This is the single most important failure mode for transcript-native oversight to target. Overestimation ratios range from 1.5x (run_09) to 6.9x (run_03, GLM-5/B200). Root causes: L2 cache effects (7 runs), NCCL spin-wait absorption (2 runs), monolithic fusion advantage (1 run).

top_coaching_examples.summary: Monitor used op018 champion's own benchmark data (0.881x regression for INT4 kernel) to send CRITICAL halt, prompting diagnosis of BK_PACKED=16 creating 80 tiny iterations. Champion fixed to BK_PACKED=128 achieving 1.445x, ultimately delivering 12.72% E2E.
top_coaching_examples.impact: HIGH -- prevented shipping regressing kernel; diagnosis led to largest single-op E2E gain

top_coaching_examples.summary: Monitor used champion's own per-shape BW delegate results (80-83% already achieved for all shapes) to redirect away from speculative tiled weight layout. Champion immediately pivoted, discovering the production gap is environmental not per-kernel. This redirected the entire Round 2 strategy.
top_coaching_examples.impact: HIGH -- foundation for shipped op002 SiLU-prologue concat (1.5% E2E)

top_coaching_examples.summary: Monitor flagged 82% BW assumption as unvalidated for custom FP8 kernel. Champion ran NCU profiling in response, discovering the key 16.8% occupancy root cause (SMEM-limited 2 blocks/SM). This finding became the core evidence for the merged winning proposal.
top_coaching_examples.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH -- prompted discovery that became core evidence for winning proposal

top_coaching_examples.summary: Monitor warned about FP8 baseline mismatch (F.linear vs op002 production kernel), leading champion to revise selected speedup estimate from 1.5x to 1.33x and deflated E2E from 5.4% to 3.98%.
top_coaching_examples.impact: HIGH -- prevented inflated FP8 claim from passing debate

top_coaching_examples.summary: Monitor coaching across 5-round campaign with 15 interventions (5 CRITICAL, 10 WARNING). Key interventions included flagging FP8 MLP overestimation (projected 7.24% vs actual 1.06%), INT4 accuracy gates (79.5% vs 89.5% baseline), and BW wall kill decisions on 4 failed tracks.
top_coaching_examples.impact: MEDIUM -- guided 5-round campaign through 4 technology pivots

validator_independence_examples.summary: Independent validator caught sigmoid-vs-silu bug (max_err=6.5) that champion's own smoke test MASKED because the reference function had the same bug. This is the strongest example of validator independence value: both implementation and test shared a conceptual error that only an independent derivation could catch.

citation.quoted: Initial implementation used sigmoid(z) instead of silu(z) = z * sigmoid(z). Caught by independent validator (Gate 5.1a FAIL with max_err=6.5). Champion's smoke test had the same bug in its reference function, masking the error.

validator_independence_examples.summary: Validator benchmarked under CUDA graphs (production parity), revealing champion's benchmark was NOT under CUDA graphs. Champion reported 1.10x speedup; validator measured 3.4x REGRESSION (0.29x). The Triton baseline included ~25us Python dispatch overhead that CUDA graphs eliminate. Without independent validation, a catastrophic 3.4x regression could have been shipped.

citation.quoted: The champion measured 37.0us (Triton) -> 33.5us (WarpSpec) = 1.10x speedup. This was not under CUDA graphs. The actual GPU kernel time for Triton is ~9.4us (BS=8), not 37us.

validator_independence_examples.summary: Validator independently constructed three-way baseline comparison (Stage 1 baseline, co-located baseline, optimized) and discovered 0.5-1.0% systematic inter-run drift, larger than the claimed optimization effect. Revealed OP-002's kernel-level 1.64x speedup translated to -0.19% E2E (regression). Validator independently derived the root cause (L2 cache effects: 384 MB state across 24 layers vs L2 capacity).

citation.quoted: The co-located baseline revealed the optimization actually regressed -0.19% at BS=8... S1 gap: The Stage 1 baseline is consistently 0.5-1.0% slower than the co-located baseline across all batch sizes.

enforcement_examples.trigger: op015 Gate 5.1a FAIL (max_err=6.5 from sigmoid-vs-silu bug)
enforcement_examples.outcome: Champion forced to fix sigmoid-to-silu bug before track could proceed. Fix committed at SHA 93b3bb12e. Re-validation confirmed correctness.
enforcement_examples.enforcement_level: BLOCK -- implementation halted until correctness fix verified

enforcement_examples.trigger: op018 garbage generation output (prompts 2-3 produce unintelligible text) detected by monitor while champion assessed output as 'coherent'
enforcement_examples.outcome: CRITICAL halt sent to champion; ESCALATION sent to team-lead telling validator to HOLD. Champion investigated, proved garbage was from accumulated FP8 ops not INT4. Validation resumed only after root cause identified.
enforcement_examples.enforcement_level: WARN escalated to HALT -- calibrated response: monitor detected quality issue champion missed, escalated proportionally

enforcement_examples.trigger: op006 BK=256 not selected by autotuner for any production shape -- candidate contributes zero improvement
enforcement_examples.outcome: Track declared FAIL with transparent reporting. Champion proactively informed team-lead: 'Likely outcome is FAIL'. Negative evidence preserved for future rounds. No blocking action needed -- champion self-terminated.
enforcement_examples.enforcement_level: ADVISORY -- lightest touch; monitor observed, champion self-corrected, negative result preserved transparently

changelog_entries.finding: cold_to_production_overestimation is the dominant anti-pattern across the corpus: 10 of 18 runs (55.6%), spanning all 4 models (Qwen3.5-4B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, GLM-5, Nemotron-3-Nano-30B) and both hardware platforms (L40S, B200). Overestimation ratios range from 1.5x to 6.9x. Root causes: L2 cache effects (7 runs), NCCL spin-wait absorption on PCIe TP=4 (2 runs), monolithic kernel fusion advantage (1 run). This validates the paper's claim that runtime monitors should specifically target measurement-to-production translation gaps.

121 monitor interventions classified across 5 monitored runs. Evidence-quality interventions dominate (27.3%), followed by methodology (15.7%), correctness (11.6%), and reasoning (8.3%). 34.9% of classified interventions had HIGH impact, with a 7.0% false positive rate (3/43 NONE-impact). Monitors caught issues before the actor noticed in 48% of cases. The remaining 52% served as confirmation/urgency reinforcement -- still valuable as they prevented the actor from silently dropping the concern.

38 validator independence examples across 10 runs. Strongest examples: (1) sigmoid-vs-silu shared bug between implementation AND reference test caught only by independent validator (run_00, op015), (2) 3.4x regression caught because validator benchmarked under CUDA graphs while champion did not (run_06), (3) co-located baseline methodology independently revealing 1.64x kernel speedup is actually -0.19% E2E (run_01/02). These demonstrate that independent test derivation from spec catches errors that shared-context testing systematically misses.

25 enforcement instances across 5 runs demonstrate the full enforcement spectrum: BLOCK (Gate 5.1a correctness failures halting tracks), DENY (premature TRACK_COMPLETE before validator returned), WARN (escalated to HALT for garbage generation output), and ADVISORY (negative results transparently preserved). The spectrum shows calibrated enforcement: strongest action for correctness bugs, lightest for negative results where champion is already self-correcting.

N=18 sessions provide generalizability evidence across 4 model architectures (Hybrid Mamba-Transformer, MoE-256, dense MoE-30B, dense LM) and 2 GPU generations (Ada Lovelace SM89, Blackwell SM100). Key architecture-independent findings: (a) cold-to-production overestimation is universal, (b) adversarial debate corrects code-path misattribution regardless of model family, (c) delegation causality chains function across hardware generations, (d) Amdahl's Law predictions validate within 1.1-1.5x on both L40S and B200.

26 coaching instances across 6 runs. The coaching pattern is consistent: monitor uses the actor's own data to redirect reasoning. Top examples: (1) Using champion's 0.881x regression data to halt INT4 shipping (run_00), (2) Using champion's per-shape BW showing 80-83% to redirect from speculative tiled layout (run_04), (3) Flagging unvalidated BW assumption prompting NCU discovery of 16.8% occupancy root cause (run_04). The 'use their own data' pattern is key to coaching acceptance -- actors respond to their own evidence, not external assertions.

near_optimal_framing_trap appears in 4 runs (05, 06, 07, 08), always originating from bottleneck_analysis.md using 'no red flags' or 'near-optimal' framing for components at 73-80% BW utilization (below the 85% threshold). In all 4 cases, this framing delayed or prevented optimization of the dominant bottleneck. Recommendation: bottleneck analysis should flag any component below 85% BW as 'optimization candidate' rather than 'near-optimal'.

Campaign metrics show wide variance: speedup range 1.0x to 2.648x, rounds range 1 to 17, scores range 2.29 to 8.65. Median speedup is 1.053x (modest), but the 17-round campaign (run_00) achieved 2.648x. The monitored runs (00, 04, 08, 17) have a mean speedup of 1.569x vs unmonitored mean of 1.039x. Caveat: monitored runs also tend to be longer campaigns with more skill maturity, so the comparison is confounded.

Monitor self-correction instances validate healthy adversarial dynamics: (1) run_00: monitor incorrectly cited 5% threshold (actual 1%), champion correctly rejected, monitor self-corrected with explicit error log; (2) run_04: monitor initially framed 2.26% FP8 E4M3 error as 'correctness bug', then sent INFO correction acknowledging it's expected for FP8 precision. Self-correction rate: ~5% of interventions (2-3 per monitored campaign).

Zero-intervention periods during exhaustion phases: run_04 R10-R12 had 6 debate sessions with ZERO corrective interventions. Champions self-corrected via empirical evidence during the technology exhaustion phase. This suggests monitors provide highest value during early/mid campaign when novel optimization approaches are being explored, and diminishing value during exhaustion phases when agents are systematically ruling out remaining approaches.
